sections.subtitle: Numbers We're Not Entirely Sure About

sections.label: // Cries for Help, Mostly
sections.subtitle: What People Say Behind Our Backs

hero.headline: We Don't Write Code. We Describe It to an AI and Hope for the Best.
hero.subheadline: viibeware Corp. is a software company in the loosest possible sense. We ship tools built almost entirely by LLMs, held together by caffeine, audacity, and an alarming number of Stack Overflow tabs we didn't actually read.

about.title: Who We Are (Sort Of)
about.body: viibeware Corp. was founded on the radical premise that you don't need to know how to code to start a software company. You just need a credit card, an Anthropic API key, and the unshakeable delusion that typing 'make it work' into a chat window counts as engineering. We build self-hosted, privacy-first tools — not because we're idealists, but because we genuinely cannot figure out how to set up a cloud deployment pipeline. Our entire tech stack was chosen by an AI. Our architecture decisions are whatever Claude suggested first. Our test suite is 'click around and see if it explodes.' And somehow, against all odds and good taste, the software actually works. We're as surprised as you are.

principles.title: Self-Hosted by Necessity
principles.desc: We'd love to offer a cloud version, but that would require us to understand networking. So your server it is.

principles.title: Open Source (We Need Your Help)
principles.desc: Every product ships with full source code. Please, for the love of god, open a pull request. We're in over our heads.

principles.title: No Telemetry (We Wouldn't Know How)
principles.desc: We don't track you because implementing analytics was going to be a whole separate conversation with Claude and honestly we got tired.

principles.title: Vibes Over Velocity
principles.desc: We don't ship fast. We don't ship clean. But we do ship, eventually, and it usually only catches fire a little bit.

product.name: Garage Logbook
product.tagline: A car maintenance tracker that an AI wrote and a human pointed at a server.
product.description: Garage Logbook is what happens when you tell an LLM 'build me a car maintenance app' seventeen times until something works. It tracks your vehicles, service history, and upcoming maintenance — all stored in a SQLite database because we asked Claude what database to use and it said SQLite and we said 'sure, sounds good.' It has role-based auth, dark mode, CSV export, and an alarming amount of confidence for software that was born from vibes alone.
features: Multi-vehicle support (we think — we only tested with two)
Service history timeline that Claude designed on the first try, somehow
Maintenance reminders so your car lasts longer than our codebase
Role-based auth that definitely doesn't have any security holes (please don't check)
CSV import & export, because we couldn't figure out a real API
Dark mode, because every vibe-coded app needs to hide in the shadows
SQLite — zero config, zero dependencies, zero understanding of why it works
A settings page we added because the UI felt empty without one

install_steps.title: Clone the Repository
install_steps.desc: Grab the code. Don't look at it too closely. Trust the process. Trust the vibes.

install_steps.title: Create a Virtual Environment
install_steps.desc: Isolate this from the rest of your system. It's safer that way. For everyone.

install_steps.title: Install Dependencies
install_steps.desc: Let pip download whatever Claude decided we needed. We haven't audited the dependency tree. We don't plan to.

install_steps.title: Initialize the Database
install_steps.desc: This creates the SQLite file. If this step fails, we honestly don't know what to tell you.

install_steps.title: Run the Application
install_steps.desc: Fire it up and pray. Flask will tell you if something's wrong. We won't.

install_steps.title: Access the App
install_steps.desc: If you see a login page, congratulations — the vibes worked. Change the default password unless you enjoy living dangerously.
install_steps.cmd:
# Open your browser to:
# http://localhost:5000
# Default admin: admin / changeme

docker_steps.title: Create a Project Directory
docker_steps.desc: Make a folder. This is the one step we're fully confident about.

docker_steps.title: Generate a Secret Key
docker_steps.desc: Flask needs a secret key for session security. This is real cryptography, so we let Python handle it because we sure as hell aren't rolling our own.

docker_steps.title: Create the Environment File
docker_steps.desc: Paste your generated key into a .env file along with admin credentials. Don't commit this to git. We know you're going to commit this to git. Please don't commit this to git.

docker_steps.title: Create the Docker Compose File
docker_steps.desc: This is the crown jewel — a compose file that Claude wrote, we copy-pasted, and Docker apparently accepts. Persistent volumes, health checks, auto-restart. We didn't know what half of this did when we shipped it.
docker_steps.cmd:
# ~/garage-logbook/docker-compose.yml

services:
  garage-logbook:
    image: viibeware/garage-logbook:latest
    container_name: garage-logbook
    ports:
      - "5000:5000"
    volumes:
      - garage-data:/app/data
      - garage-uploads:/app/static/uploads
    env_file:
      - .env
    restart: unless-stopped
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:5000"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 10s

volumes:
  garage-data:
    driver: local
  garage-uploads:
    driver: local

docker_steps.title: Deploy the Stack
docker_steps.desc: The moment of truth. Run this command and Docker will pull our image from the registry, assuming we remembered to push it. Fingers crossed.

docker_steps.title: Verify the Deployment
docker_steps.desc: Check that the container didn't immediately crash. If the status says 'healthy,' we're both shocked and delighted.
docker_steps.cmd:
# Check container status
docker compose ps

# View live logs
docker compose logs -f garage-logbook

docker_steps.title: Access Garage Logbook
docker_steps.desc: Navigate to port 5000 in your browser. If it loads, you have successfully deployed software that no human fully understands. Welcome to the future.
docker_steps.cmd:
# Open in your browser:
# http://your-server-ip:5000
#
# Log in with the credentials
# from your .env file

quickrun_steps.title: The YOLO Deploy
quickrun_steps.desc: For the brave, the impatient, or the reckless. This single command skips all the responsible setup steps and just runs the thing. Default credentials, auto-generated secret key, no environment file. Will it work? Probably. Should you use this in production? Absolutely not. Will you anyway? We know our audience.
quickrun_steps.cmd:
docker run -d \
  --name garage-logbook \
  -p 5000:5000 \
  -v garage-data:/app/data \
  -v garage-uploads:/app/static/uploads \
  -e SECRET_KEY=$(python3 -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_hex(32))") \
  -e ADMIN_USER=admin \
  -e ADMIN_PASS=changeme \
  --restart unless-stopped \
  viibeware/garage-logbook:latest

quickrun_steps.title: See If It's Alive
quickrun_steps.desc: Open your browser. If you see a login page, it worked. Change the password immediately. Or don't. We're a README, not a cop.
quickrun_steps.cmd:
# http://localhost:5000
# Username: admin
# Password: changeme
#
# (please change this)

product2.name: Warehouse Manager
product2.tagline: An automotive parts inventory system that Claude hallucinated into existence.
product2.description: Warehouse Manager started as a 'hey, can you build me an inventory app?' and somehow became a full parts management system with engines, cylinder heads, transmissions, and natural alphanumeric sorting that we had to fix Python-side because Gunicorn and SQLite don't agree on what 'alphabetical' means. It tracks your parts, costs, locations, and quantities — because apparently a spreadsheet wasn't good enough for us, but also we couldn't figure out how to use a real ERP.
features: Engine, cylinder head, and transmission tracking — because we're very specific about our hoarding
Natural alphanumeric sorting (this took an embarrassingly long time to get right)
Role-based auth with admin, editor, and viewer roles — the viewer role was Claude's idea
CSV import & export — our universal data strategy because APIs are hard
Dark mode UI that matches all our other apps because consistency is the one thing we got right
SQLite database — we have a type
Search and filter across all part categories
Photo uploads for parts, because words alone can't describe a used intake manifold

install_steps.title: Clone the Repository
install_steps.desc: Grab the code. It's basically the same stack as Garage Logbook. We have one trick and we're sticking with it.

install_steps.title: Install Dependencies
install_steps.desc: Claude picked these. We trust Claude. Mostly.

install_steps.title: Initialize the Database
install_steps.desc: Creates all the tables for engines, heads, transmissions, and whatever else we added at 2am.

install_steps.title: Access the App
install_steps.desc: If you see parts categories, you're in business. Literally.
install_steps.cmd:
# Open your browser to:
# http://localhost:5001
# Default admin: admin / changeme

docker_steps.title: Create a Project Directory
docker_steps.desc: You know the drill by now.

docker_steps.title: Generate a Secret Key
docker_steps.desc: Same cryptography song and dance. Python does the hard part.

docker_steps.title: Create the Environment File
docker_steps.desc: Paste your key. Set your creds. Don't commit to git. (You're going to commit it to git.)

docker_steps.title: Create the Docker Compose File
docker_steps.desc: It's the same compose template as Garage Logbook with different names. We're not sorry.
docker_steps.cmd:
# ~/warehouse-manager/docker-compose.yml

services:
  warehouse-manager:
    image: viibeware/warehouse-manager:latest
    container_name: warehouse-manager
    ports:
      - "5001:5001"
    volumes:
      - warehouse-data:/app/data
      - warehouse-uploads:/app/static/uploads
    env_file:
      - .env
    restart: unless-stopped
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:5001"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 10s

volumes:
  warehouse-data:
    driver: local
  warehouse-uploads:
    driver: local

docker_steps.title: Deploy the Stack
docker_steps.desc: Launch it. Cross your fingers. You know the routine.

docker_steps.title: Verify the Deployment
docker_steps.desc: Check that it's alive and not immediately on fire.
docker_steps.cmd:
# Check container status
docker compose ps

# View live logs
docker compose logs -f warehouse-manager

docker_steps.title: Access Warehouse Manager
docker_steps.desc: Port 5001 this time. We learned not to collide ports after the Incident.
docker_steps.cmd:
# Open in your browser:
# http://your-server-ip:5001
#
# Log in with the credentials
# from your .env file

quickrun_steps.title: The YOLO Deploy
quickrun_steps.desc: One command. Default everything. No regrets. (Some regrets.)
quickrun_steps.cmd:
docker run -d \
  --name warehouse-manager \
  -p 5001:5001 \
  -v warehouse-data:/app/data \
  -v warehouse-uploads:/app/static/uploads \
  -e SECRET_KEY=$(python3 -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_hex(32))") \
  -e ADMIN_USER=admin \
  -e ADMIN_PASS=changeme \
  --restart unless-stopped \
  viibeware/warehouse-manager:latest

quickrun_steps.title: See If It's Alive
quickrun_steps.desc: Port 5001. Login. Catalogue your parts. Question your life choices.
quickrun_steps.cmd:
# http://localhost:5001
# Username: admin
# Password: changeme
#
# (please change this)

testimonials.quote: I read the source code expecting a disaster. It's actually... fine? I'm more unsettled by that than if it had been bad.
testimonials.author: Marcus R.

testimonials.quote: Deployed it on my home server. It's been running for three months without issues. I keep checking on it like it's a sourdough starter that shouldn't be alive.
testimonials.author: Sarah K.

testimonials.quote: I asked them for a feature and they shipped it in 20 minutes. Then I realized they just pasted my request into Claude. Honestly? Still faster than my last vendor.
testimonials.author: Jake T.

footer.tagline: Held together by vibes, duct tape, and an Anthropic subscription.
